does: This paper develops a robotic fire risk detection system combining knowledge graphs with large language models and multimodal models. The authors construct a domain-specific knowledge graph by leveraging LLMs to encode fire prevention guidelines and robotic emergency response procedures, then propose the Insights-on-Graph (IOG) framework that integrates this structured knowledge with Large Multimodal Models processing real-time scene imagery. The approach generates perception-driven risk graphs from visual input to detect fire hazards early and produce interpretable emergency response recommendations for robotic task planning and execution in fire scenarios.
connects: This work builds on the integration of knowledge graphs with neural language models, extending prior research on knowledge-enhanced reasoning systems toward robotics applications. It combines structured knowledge representation—a technique proven valuable in question-answering and reasoning tasks—with recent advances in LLMs and vision-language models that can ground symbolic knowledge in perceptual data. The paper responds to longstanding challenges in robotic situational awareness and emergency response planning by addressing the gap between raw sensor perception and semantic understanding through graph-based reasoning, borrowing from work on neuro-symbolic AI that marries symbolic knowledge with neural learning.
enables: This framework enables next-generation emergency response robots with richer semantic understanding of fire risk, moving beyond simple sensor-based detection to knowledge-grounded reasoning about complex hazardous scenarios. The approach opens pathways for robots to generate interpretable decision explanations to human operators, improving trust and oversight in high-stakes environments. Future work could extend the method to multi-robot coordination by sharing risk graphs across platforms, adapt the framework to other disaster types by constructing domain-specific KGs, and integrate active learning mechanisms where robots iteratively refine fire domain knowledge through real-world rescue experience, enhancing robustness in novel fire scenarios.
